I'm reading https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/stable.html. It poses some important considerations and suggests to test all minor Python versions. According to psutil's `setup.py` the most minor support Python version is 3.4:

```python
    if setuptools is not None:
        kwargs.update(
            python_requires=">=2.7, !=3.0.*, !=3.1.*, !=3.2.*, !=3.3.*",
            extras_require=extras_require,
            zip_safe=False,
        )
```

As such I wonder: 
* would it make sense for `build.yml` to test 3.4? 
* what python versions are we testing right now?  I see that windows has `["3.7", "3.8", "3.9", "3.10", "~3.11.0-0"]` but I'm unclear about the other platforms.

Again, thanks for this great work!

Hello,

Thanks for all the work and the major modifications in your code, it is now in my opinion in a much better shape and we are approaching a merge. 

Still I have a few important modifications now that I better understand what your proposed solvers actually do.  I know it can get tiresome but POT is becoming very large and we need to be very careful with positioning new API and solvers.

My understanding is that they are MMOT solvers for marginal distribution that have a support on a regular 1D grid and using a specific ground metric (max-min that has monge property) that corresponds to the absolute value loss/EMD ground metric with two marginals.

This is very interesting and I want it in POT but it also means that we cannot use the very general names of the method that you chose in your published paper because it would lead to much confusion for POT users. So I proposed more precise names below that describe I think better what is solved and done. I provide more specific comments below (for instance having mathematical object follow the name of function parameters).

On a more scientific question I am surprised by the shape or your "barycenter" that is actually from my understanding a convergence point for the minimization of your MMOT formulation. It seems to be very similar to L2 (average) barycenters especially compared to the LP solution. Do you have an intuition why?

```Python
out = exposure.match_histograms(data.chelsea(), data.astronaut())
```

This requires creating a datasource each time we make this check, this is a lengthy process and we should avoid doing it as much as possible (hence why we are moving towards lazy intialization of datasources)

Unless there's a good reason we want this (business wise) this type of error doesn't take priority over any other type of error.

So I will say that for now I'd want to reject this change

I'm sorry but I think a lot of work remains to make this useful for LightGBM's users. Right now, this PR adds support for only a very limited interface, and with very little documentation. Even this PR's description doesn't really explain how this change improves LightGBM.

1. Why is this PR only adding support for reading in positions from a text file, and only for the case where the training data is also provided via a file?
     - I'd expect to be able to provide these positions the same way as other "metadata" like `initial_score` and `weight` are provided, and for how I provide it to not depend on how I provide the training dataset
2. Can you please add documentation ([here, maybe](https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/9f78cceee4911dd56f4635dfd36d4482363db5aa/docs/Advanced-Topics.rst#L37)) covering this functionality? for example...
    - plain-language explanation of this functionality, the benefit of it, and when users should use it
        - (including links to relevant literature)
    - the fact that position bias factors can only be supplied via a file, only when training on a dataset also provided in a file,
    - the fact that that file must exactly be named `{name-of-train-data-file}.position`
    - the expected content of that file like:
        - dimensions (one line per group in `group`? one line per observation in the training data? something else?)
        - values (has to start with prefix `pos_`? what do different values mean?)
3. Does this implementation assume that all the positions are strings beginning with the prefix `"pos_*"`? (forgive me, I'm not very familiar with C++ so it's hard for me to tell)
    - if so... why is that preferable to providing integers? Integers would be much less expensive to store (both on disk and in-memory) and therefore less likely to cause memory issues for users.

Thanks for tackling this @viren-nadkarni ! 👍 Great catch on removal of `DEFAULT_REGION` - this dates back to ancient times when LS was not fully multi-region capable yet. :)

Regarding removal of `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`/`AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, not sure if this is a great user experience - so far, the premise was always that `awslocal` should ideally work out of the box, using sensible defaults where needed. I can see that this change could create some headache and break quite some existing pipelines out there. 🤔 (At least we shouldn't introduce it with a patch/minor relase imo.)
```
$ awslocal s3 mb s3://foo
make_bucket failed: s3://foo Unable to locate credentials
```

Could we maybe attempt to create a boto3 client, which will go through the credentials resolver chain, and then selectively only set individual environment variable defaults if the client cannot be created? Something like the following (not fully fleshed out, just conceptually):
```
try:
    boto3.client("sts").get_caller_identity()
except botocore.exceptions.NoCredentialsError:
    env_dict["AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID"] = "test"
    env_dict["AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"] = "test"

# optionally, we could also add a fallback for the region (but maybe less important than the credentials):
try:
    boto3.client("sqs").list_queues()
except botocore.exceptions.NoRegionError:
    env_dict["AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"] = "..."
```

This will add a tiny fraction to the runtime of `awslocal` commands, but I think it would make for a better user experience. Also, this way we could still make use of profiles and `~/.aws/config`. If we're concerned about the additional runtime overhead, we could make the checks configurable (with an option to switch off the setting of defaults).

Thoughts?

Only one thing that worries me a little about looping/waiting for results this way.
We already have config settings `min_time_s_per_iteration`, `min_train_timesteps_per_iteration`, etc..
These settings make sure that "1 iteration" has a minimum length and thus normally returns something.
Using these "min"-settings has another problem currently though, which is that in case we do several calls to `training_step` in one iteration (b/c the minimum count/time is not hit in a single call), we only report the latest results, not an accumulation of all encountered results.

@ericl @jianoaix In general, consistently resetting the index was a very early design decision, where we decided that a block's index should represent the dataset record order; this means that we need to reset the index whenever we slice, concatenate, or shuffle blocks, with the end-result being that each block is locally ordered, and if all blocks are concatenated together (e.g. via `ds.to_pandas()`), the resulting index would give the global dataset order. This was under the assumption that the user would _not_ be encoding application-level identifiers into the index, since we hadn't encountered that use case at the time.

@jianoaix This particular index drop was added because of this issue: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/27019/commits/a48e34d25477f4d0f90b7bb7ac4909c8a042b696#r931308133.

If we're going to try to maintain the index, we can't only make the change on concatenation; we would need to do a global audit of all other places we reset the index, e.g.:
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L147
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L154
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L167

And before we decide to try to maintain the index, we need to ensure that index-aware operations (i.e. operations that automatically align on indices) will not now give incorrect results, and we might want to only preserve the original index if the index is not the default sequential ordering index (assuming there's an easy way to check that).

> The thing here that I don't think it is ok is the upgrade behavior and tests, so would love the feedback! Most specifically, I don't think that I should be calling `cparser.llhttp_resume_after_upgrade(self._cparser)`, but expose this as a method like `def resume_after_upgrade(self)`, but left it there to hear your opinion.

Looks like what `llhttp_resume_after_upgrade()` does is quite simple - it reset the parser's internal error state if it was paused for upgrade. So according to another doc you quoted:

> ```
>  * NOTE: if this function ever returns a non-pause type error, it will continue
>  * to return the same error upon each successive call up until `llhttp_init()`
>  * is called.
> llhttp_errno_t llhttp_execute(llhttp_t* parser, const char* data, size_t len);
> ```

It's all about how the parser behaves after detecting an upgrade. In short, I think you're right to call `llhttp_resume_after_upgrade()` in `feed_data()` (the only missing step in your PR is to calculate the pointer's difference, see below), or successive calls to `feed_data()` will raise `HttpParserUpgrade` errors, which is inconsistent with the current released httptools. What's more, according to the docs of `llhttp_get_error_pos()`:

> Returns the pointer to the last parsed byte before the returned error. The
> pointer is relative to the `data` argument of `llhttp_execute()`.
> 
> Note: this method might be useful for counting the number of parsed bytes.

There's no guarantee that the 2nd call to `feed_data()` is using the same `data` parameter - in other words, the return value of `llhttp_get_error_pos()` is considered an invalid pointer without the context of the first call to `feed_data()`, so we should calculate the number of parsed bytes while we can.

For the behavior of calling `feed_data()` again after handling `HttpParserUpgrade` errors, we should add a test for it, even though this is a rare use case.

TL;DR: Please see suggested patch below:

```patch
diff --git a/httptools/parser/parser.pyx b/httptools/parser/parser.pyx
index 6656bef..3158d0e 100644
--- a/httptools/parser/parser.pyx
+++ b/httptools/parser/parser.pyx
@@ -161,13 +161,15 @@ cdef class HttpParser:
     def feed_data(self, data):
         cdef:
             size_t data_len
-            cparser.llhttp_errno_t nb
+            cparser.llhttp_errno_t err
             Py_buffer *buf
+            bint owning_buf = False
+            char* err_pos

         if PyMemoryView_Check(data):
             buf = PyMemoryView_GET_BUFFER(data)
             data_len = <size_t>buf.len
-            nb = cparser.llhttp_execute(
+            err = cparser.llhttp_execute(
                 self._cparser,
                 <char*>buf.buf,
                 data_len)
@@ -175,21 +177,34 @@ cdef class HttpParser:
         else:
             buf = &self.py_buf
             PyObject_GetBuffer(data, buf, PyBUF_SIMPLE)
+            owning_buf = True
             data_len = <size_t>buf.len

-            nb = cparser.llhttp_execute(
+            err = cparser.llhttp_execute(
                 self._cparser,
                 <char*>buf.buf,
                 data_len)

-            PyBuffer_Release(buf)
-
-        if self._cparser.upgrade == 1 and nb == cparser.HPE_PAUSED_UPGRADE:
-            read_bytes = cparser.llhttp_get_error_pos(self._cparser)
-            cparser.llhttp_resume_after_upgrade(self._cparser)
-            raise HttpParserUpgrade(read_bytes)
-
-        if nb != cparser.HPE_OK:
+        try:
+            if self._cparser.upgrade == 1 and err == cparser.HPE_PAUSED_UPGRADE:
+                err_pos = cparser.llhttp_get_error_pos(self._cparser)
+
+                # Immediately free the parser from "error" state, simulating
+                # http-parser behavior here because 1) we never had the API to
+                # allow users manually "resume after upgrade", and 2) the use
+                # case for resuming parsing is very rare.
+                cparser.llhttp_resume_after_upgrade(self._cparser)
+
+                # The err_pos here is specific for the input buf. So if we ever
+                # switch to the llhttp behavior (re-raise HttpParserUpgrade for
+                # successive calls to feed_data() until resume_after_upgrade is
+                # called), we have to store the result and keep our own state.
+                raise HttpParserUpgrade(err_pos - <char*>buf.buf)
+        finally:
+            if owning_buf:
+                PyBuffer_Release(buf)
+
+        if err != cparser.HPE_OK:
             ex = parser_error_from_errno(
                 self._cparser,
                 <cparser.llhttp_errno_t> self._cparser.error)
diff --git a/tests/test_parser.py b/tests/test_parser.py
index 101da22..cd6f59c 100644
--- a/tests/test_parser.py
+++ b/tests/test_parser.py
@@ -332,11 +332,11 @@ class TestRequestParser(unittest.TestCase):
         try:
             p.feed_data(UPGRADE_REQUEST1)
         except httptools.HttpParserUpgrade as ex:
-            offset = len(ex.args[0])
+            offset = ex.args[0]
         else:
             self.fail('HttpParserUpgrade was not raised')

-        self.assertEqual(UPGRADE_REQUEST1[-offset:], b'Hot diggity dogg')
+        self.assertEqual(UPGRADE_REQUEST1[offset:], b'Hot diggity dogg')

         self.assertEqual(headers, {
             b'Sec-WebSocket-Key2': b'12998 5 Y3 1  .P00',
@@ -347,6 +347,11 @@ class TestRequestParser(unittest.TestCase):
             b'Host': b'example.com',
             b'Upgrade': b'WebSocket'})

+        # The parser can be used again for further parsing - this is a legacy
+        # behavior from the time we were still using http-parser.
+        p.feed_data(CHUNKED_REQUEST1_1)
+        self.assertEqual(p.get_method(), b'POST')
+
     def test_parser_request_upgrade_flag(self):
```

This looks great! One small tweak to the test, but otherwise everything looks perfect.

In terms of the JSON deserialization, the [implementation](https://github.com/dmlc/treelite/blob/mainline/src/frontend/xgboost_json.cc) is all in RapidJSON. I don't know if you've worked with that library before, but the basic pattern is that you have a handler that moves through each JSON element and delegates parsing to whatever more specialized handler is currently on top of the "stack" of delegator handlers. Typically when you start a new object, you push a specialized handler for that object onto the stack, and when you reach the end of that object, you pop the stack, returning control to the handler for the enclosing object.

So, you'll need to make 2 changes in the xgboost_json code to support dart. The simplest one is just adding `"dart"` as an allowed name [here](https://github.com/dmlc/treelite/blob/mainline/src/frontend/xgboost_json.cc#L337). The more important change will be adding a `GradientBoosterHandler::StartArray` method. When an array is encountered inside a `GradientBoosterHandler` object, you'll want to push a new handler onto the stack which will store the contents of that array to some temporary vector which can then be used to modify the leaf values as you've done with the binary case.

The most analogous example of this in the existing code is [here](https://github.com/dmlc/treelite/blob/mainline/src/frontend/xgboost_json.cc#L405-L408). In this case, all we need to do is store the version information and then check it [here](https://github.com/dmlc/treelite/blob/mainline/src/frontend/xgboost_json.cc#L433). In your case, you may need to think about where to store that vector a bit more in order to access it when the leaf values are constructed (or modify them after parsing is fully complete).

Let me know if that gives you enough to start on it or if you'd like some more detailed help.

>  This maintains the current behavior if someone sets the agentControllerType to stochastic, but doesn't set the agentMode="locobot".

Hmm.. do we really want this? The stochastic controller is inherently tied to the LoCoBot, down to the point where the agent is unable to MoveLeft, MoveRight, Crouch, Stand, or do any type of interaction. So, trying to use the stochastic agent controller type with the default agent or the drone will lead to many confusing breaking points (and is the reason `agentControllerType` remains undocumented, as it is implied from `agentMode`).

If somebody wants stochastic actions with some other agent type, I think they should control the amount of stochasticity from Python. This will also allow them to sample whatever random variable distribution they want, instead of just using Gaussians. For instance, one may have:
```python
from ai2thor.controller import Controller
import numpy as np

controller = Controller(snapToGrid=False)

move_size = 0.25
for _ in range(20):
    noise = np.random.exponential()  # replace with your favorite continuous random variable
    step_size = move_size + noise
    controller.step(action="MoveAhead", moveMagnitude=step_size)
```

Here, I think I would prefer to put all of StochasticRemoteFPSAgentController into LocobotFPSAgentController, and have LocobotFPSAgentController inherit from Base.

I appreciate the refactor into multiple files!  There is certainly room for further improvements to the codebase in this service, but this is a nice start.

I think the scopes question is an important one.  The GitHub service automatically has `assume:repo:github.com/*`, so whatever scopes `queue:rerun-task:...` that implies will apply to re-running a task as this PR is currently written -- which is probably both too broad (one project can rerun another project's tasks) and too narrow (if no role has the necessary `queue:rerun-task:..` scope, the rerun will fail).

The situation is both a little bit risky, because _lots_ of people can click that rerun button in GitHub, and a little bit safe, because it's difficult to forge a GitHub webhook and even more difficult to forge a pulse message, so it's reasonable to assume that the suite/run ID's are something the originating user had access to.

> Note that, as of the current state of this PR, Context.permissions may include voice permissions in text channels and the like due to how Interaction.permissions works. I can add in a check for the channel type and forcibly deny unrelated permissions if desired.

This is a bit tricky because this deviation in behaviour, combined with the fact that you changed the checks means that in hybrid_commands the checks will pass or fail depending on whether it's an interaction or not. For example, historically things like `@has_permissions(mute_members=True)` have always failed but now it depends on the context. I consider this to be a massive gotcha and a weird inconsistency.

As you've noted, there are a few solutions to this problem.
1. We keep the functions as-is and just document the inconsistency more prominently
2. We modify the behaviour of `Interaction.permissions` to fit within the confines of the library.

I have no strong opinion on here except that for consistency sake checks should stick to using the regular `permissions_for` method because this deviation would be too surprising. I suspect the easiest route would be to just document the discrepancy with a relevant example (such as e.g. `connect` not being available in text channels).

I think this is okay as a fix for now, but I think in the long term we can actually move away from the lazy checkpointing logic + the marker.

Future checkpointing logic:
1. Worker pushes to persistent storage (driver, NFS, cloud are all the same).
2. Worker passes metadata to the coordinator. This is a lightweight `CheckpointMetadata` (<-- a made up class that's similar to today's `_TrackedCheckpoint`, except its data is always just a path of where the checkpoint lives, at the storage path).
3. Then, there's no need to distinguish between passing within a node vs. between nodes, since the metadata is so small.
The behaviour of the property `category` of the ResultSummary is wrong, it should be `UNKNOWN` when the category is not present in the metadata.

I'm fine with this, it's a small change, but are we sure this is an actual gain?
My understanding:
* The code as-is only copies references (i.e. pointers in disguise), the only `string` copy is at the end when `format` is copied to the `buffer_info` member.
* With the new version, the copy is in the two ctors, then there a a couple moves for the string to land in the member.

Now guessing:
It's much easier for an optimizer to get rid of the reference/pointer copies, than it is to optimize out the moves (the string move ctor code probably isn't completely trivial, but I didn't go to look).
Does that make sense?

Thanks for looking into this! But I don't think the proposed change is correct.

When using, for example, `DaskLGBMRegressor`, `lightgbm.dask` will create one task running `LGBMRegressor.fit()` on each Dask worker.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/2f5d898520dfa7a09f9f73794e5a0586eea1d4f5/python-package/lightgbm/dask.py#L765-L767

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/2f5d898520dfa7a09f9f73794e5a0586eea1d4f5/python-package/lightgbm/dask.py#L164

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/2f5d898520dfa7a09f9f73794e5a0586eea1d4f5/python-package/lightgbm/dask.py#L319-L329

At that moment, that evaluation function will be called on only _local_ data in each worker process, which will be numpy arrays, not Dask arrays.

Here's some sample code proving that. Note that it includes an `assert` which will fail if `y_true` is anything other than a numpy array.

<details><summary>(sample code)</summary>

```python
import dask.array as da
import numpy as np
from dask.distributed import Client, LocalCluster, wait
from lightgbm.dask import DaskLGBMRegressor
from sklearn.datasets import make_regression

# set  up Dask cluster
n_workers = 2
cluster = LocalCluster(n_workers=n_workers)
client = Client(cluster)
client.wait_for_workers(n_workers)
print(f"View the dashboard: {cluster.dashboard_link}")

# create training data and an additional eval set
def _make_dataset(n_samples):
    X, y = make_regression(n_samples=n_samples)
    dX = da.from_array(X, chunks=(1000, X.shape[1]))
    dy = da.from_array(y, chunks=1000)
    return dX, dy

# training data
dX, dy = _make_dataset(10_000)

# eval data
dX_e, dy_e = _make_dataset(2_000)

def _custom_metric(y_true, y_pred):
    metric_name = "custom_metric"
    is_higher_better = False
    metric_value = 0.708
    assert str(type(y_true)) == "<class 'numpy.ndarray'>"
    return metric_name, metric_value, is_higher_better

dask_reg = DaskLGBMRegressor(
    client=client,
    objective="regression_l2",
    tree_learner="data"
)
dask_reg.fit(
    X=dX,
    y=dy,
    eval_set=[
        (dX, dy),
        (dX_e, dy_e)
    ],
    eval_metric = ["rmse", _custom_metric]
)

# confirm that custom metric function was actually called
# (should be an array of value 0.708)
print(dask_reg.evals_result_["valid_0"]["custom_metric"])
```

</details>
